David, being at rest in his palace, considered how he might best use his leisure and prosperity for the service of God. He decided to build a temple for the ark. Nathan did not speak here as a prophet, but as a godly man, encouraging David with his private judgment. We should do all we can to encourage and promote the good purposes and designs of others, and, as we have opportunity, to advance a good work.

Blessings are promised to David’s family and descendants. These promises relate to Solomon, David's immediate successor, and the royal line of Judah. But they also relate to Christ, who is often called David and the Son of David. God gave him all power in heaven and earth, with authority to execute judgment. He was to build the gospel temple, a house for God’s name; the spiritual temple of true believers, to be a habitation of God through the Spirit. The establishing of his house, his throne, and his kingdom forever can apply only to Christ and his kingdom: David's house and kingdom ended long ago. Committing iniquity cannot apply to the Messiah himself, but to his spiritual seed; true believers have weaknesses, for which they must expect to be corrected, though they are not cast off.

David’s prayer is full of heartfelt affection toward God. He had low thoughts of his own merits. All we have must be looked upon as divine gifts. He speaks very highly and honorably of the Lord's favors to him. Considering what the character and condition of man is, we may be amazed that God should deal with him as he does. The promise of Christ includes all; if the Lord God is ours, what more can we ask or think? He knows us better than we know ourselves; therefore let us be satisfied with what he has done for us. What can we say more for ourselves in our prayers than God has said for us in his promises? David ascribes all to the free grace of God: both the great things he had done for him, and the great things he had made known to him. All was for his word's sake, that is, for the sake of Christ the eternal Word. Many, when they go to pray, have their hearts searching, but David’s heart was found; that is, it was fixed, gathered in from its wanderings, entirely engaged to the duty, and employed in it. That prayer which is from the tongue only will not please God; it must be found in the heart; that must be lifted up and poured out before God. He builds his faith and hopes to succeed upon the sureness of God’s promise. David prays for the performance of the promise. With God, saying and doing are not two things, as they often are with men; God will do what he has said. The promises of God are not made to us by name, as to David, but they belong to all who believe in Jesus Christ and plead them in his name.
